topics.title: Introduction to Biomedical Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Biomedical Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Introduction to Biomedical Signals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Signal:** A *biomedical signal* is a time-varying or spatially-varying measurable bio-physical quantity, originating from a living biological system (human, animal, or plant), that carries diagnostic, therapeutic, or monitoring information about the state of that system.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Outcome-Based Education / NEP 2020 framework), biomedical signals are formally classified under the discipline of **Biomedical Engineering** and **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**. They serve as the fundamental carriers of physiological information extracted via transducers or electrodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Anchors (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - **Signal:** A function that conveys information about the state or behavior of a physical system.
> - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** The systematic application of signal processing methodologies (filtering, spectral analysis, feature extraction) to biosignals for clinical interpretation.

---

### Intuitive Analogy: The Body as a Symphony Orchestra

Imagine the human body as a **grand symphony orchestra** performing in a concert hall:

- Each instrument (heart, brain, lungs, muscles) continuously produces its own distinct sound.
- A **biomedical signal** is essentially the electrical, mechanical, or chemical "sound" emitted by each organ.
- The **electrocardiogram (ECG)** is the percussion of the heart's rhythm.
- The **electroencephalogram (EEG)** is the gentle hum of the brain's cortical activity.
- A **physician/biomedical engineer** acts like the conductor, who must isolate a single instrument's sound (filtering noise), recognize a wrong note (abnormality detection), and predict the next movement (prognosis).
- **Noise** in this analogy is the audience murmuring, the air-conditioner humming, and other instruments bleeding through.

> [!TIP]
> If you can think of biomedical signals as "messages from your body organs," then signal processing is simply the art of *reading, cleaning, and interpreting those messages* to keep you healthy.

---

### Standard Physical Constants & Vital Metrics (Highlighted for Quick Recall)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Action Potential Propagation Velocity:** ≈ **1–120 m/s** (depending on nerve fibre type).
> - **Standard ECG Lead-II Bandwidth:** **0.05 – 100 Hz** (clinical standard).
> - **Standard ECG Amplitude Range:** **0.5 – 5 mV**.
> - **Standard EEG Amplitude Range:** **10 – 300 µV** (extremely weak → high-gain amplifiers needed).
> - **Sampling Rate (Nyquist Criterion for ECG):** **≥ 250 Hz** (clinical standard).
> - **Cardiac Cycle Duration:** ≈ **0.8 seconds** at **75 BPM (Beats Per Minute)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Biomedical Signals

Biomedical signals are broadly classified into **six primary categories** based on the physical/chemical nature of the measured variable. This is a *high-frequency board question* in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

### 2.1.1 Bioelectric Signals

- **Origin:** Generated by the **electrochemical activity** of excitable cells (neurons, cardiac myocytes, muscle fibres).
- **Mechanism:** Ionic current flow → potential difference measurable at the body surface.
- **Examples:**
  - **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** — heart's electrical activity.
  - **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** — brain's electrical activity.
  - **EMG (Electromyogram)** — skeletal muscle electrical activity.
  - **EOG (Electrooculogram)** — eye-movement potentials.
  - **ERG (Electroretinogram)** — retinal response to light.
  - **EGG (Electrogastrogram)** — stomach's electrical rhythm.

### 2.1.2 Biomagnetic Signals

- **Origin:** Magnetic fields produced by bioelectric currents (per *Biot–Savart Law*).
- **Key Advantage:** Contactless measurement (no skin-electrode interface).
- **Examples:** **MCG (Magnetocardiogram)**, **MEG (Magnetoencephalogram)**.
- **Magnitudes:** Extremely weak — in the order of **pico-Tesla (10⁻¹² T)** to **femto-Tesla (10⁻¹⁵ T)** → require **SQUID** magnetometers.

### 2.1.3 Biochemical Signals

- **Origin:** Concentration of chemical species in body fluids (blood, sweat, saliva).
- **Examples:** **pH of blood**, **Partial Pressure of Oxygen (pO₂)**, **Partial Pressure of CO₂ (pCO₂)**, **Glucose concentration**, **Electrolyte concentrations** (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺).

### 2.1.4 Biomechanical Signals

- **Origin:** Mechanical motion, force, displacement, pressure, or flow.
- **Examples:** **Blood Pressure (BP)**, **Blood Flow Velocity**, **Joint Displacement**, **Respiratory Airflow**, **Gait signals**.

### 2.1.5 Bioacoustic Signals

- **Origin:** Sounds or vibrations produced by biological structures.
- **Examples:** **PCG (Phonocardiogram — heart sounds)**, **Lung sounds (breath sounds)**, **Fetal heart sounds**, **Speech signals**.

### 2.1.6 Bio-optical / Photonic Signals

- **Origin:** Light interaction (absorption, reflection, scattering) with tissue.
- **Examples:** **Photoplethysmogram (PPG)**, **Pulse oximetry signals**, **Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS)**.

### 2.1.7 Bio-thermal Signals

- **Origin:** Heat distribution across body surfaces.
- **Example:** **Infrared thermography**, skin temperature mapping.

---

## 2.2 Deterministic vs Stochastic Biomedical Signals (KTU Favourite)

| Property | Deterministic Signal | Stochastic Signal |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Behaviour | Repeatable, predictable | Random, statistical |
| Mathematical model | Explicit $x(t)$ function | Described by probability distribution |
| Examples in biomedicine | Synthetic ECG simulator output | Real ECG contaminated by muscle artefact, EEG background noise |

---

## 2.3 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time vs Digital Signals

- **Continuous-Time (Analog) Signal:** Defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ → example: $x(t) = \sin(2\pi f t)$.
- **Discrete-Time Signal:** Defined only at discrete instants $t = nT_s$ → $x[n] = x(nT_s)$.
- **Digital Signal:** Discrete in time **and** amplitude (quantized) → computer-storable.

> [!NOTE]
> The **sampling theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)** is non-negotiable: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. For ECG, $f_{max} \approx 100$ Hz, hence $f_s \geq 200$ Hz; in practice, **250 Hz, 360 Hz, or 500 Hz** are clinical standards.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Hz** — Nyquist criterion |
| Discrete mean (DC value) | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]$ | — |
| Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x[n] - \mu)^{2}$ | signal power spread |
| Standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$ | — |
| Signal Energy | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | finite only if signal decays |
| Average Signal Power | $P = \lim\limits_{N \to \infty}\dfrac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | for power signals |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $\mathrm{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | **dB** |
| SNR (amplitude form) | $\mathrm{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)$ | **dB** |
| Root Mean Square (RMS) | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x[n]^{2}}$ | — |
| Correlation coefficient | $\rho_{xy} = \dfrac{\sum x[n]\, y[n]}{\sqrt{\sum x[n]^{2}\,\sum y[n]^{2}}}$ | ranges in $[-1, +1]$ |
| Convolution (discrete) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$ | LTI system output |
| Heart Rate (from R-R) | $\mathrm{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{R-R}}$ **bpm** | $T_{R-R}$ in seconds |
| Period of ECG (75 bpm) | $T = \dfrac{60}{75} = 0.8$ s | standard rest case |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility Snapshot:**
> - **SNR** decides the choice of amplifier (low-noise instrumentation amp like AD620).
> - **Energy/Power** decide whether to design an *energy* or *power* filter.
> - **Mean & Variance** are foundational features fed to **machine-learning classifiers** for arrhythmia detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete-Time Signal Power (KTU Board Standard)

For a deterministic periodic discrete signal $x[n]$ of period $N$, the **average power** per sample is defined as:

$$
P = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}
$$

### Step 1 — Definition of Instantaneous Power

For any discrete signal, the squared magnitude represents instantaneous power:
$$
P_{\text{inst}}[n] = \vert x[n] \vert^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Summation over One Period

The total energy in one period of length $N$ is:
$$
E_{\text{period}} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Time-Averaging

Power = Energy per unit sample interval. Since we have $N$ samples covering one period, average power becomes:
$$
P = \frac{E_{\text{period}}}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Special Case: Sinusoidal Biomedical Carrier

Let $x[n] = A \sin(\omega n)$, where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{f_s}$.

Using the identity $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$
P = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega n) = \frac{A^{2}}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\frac{1 - \cos(2\omega n)}{2}
$$

For a complete period (or $N$ large enough), $\sum \cos(2\omega n) \to 0$, hence:

$$
P = \frac{A^{2}}{2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The average power of any sinusoidal biosignal is independent of frequency and equals **half the squared peak amplitude**. This is why ECG amplifiers are rated in **Vrms** rather than Vpeak.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Nyquist Sampling Rate for ECG

### Step 1 — Identify Highest Frequency Component

ECG bandwidth: $f_{max} = 100$ Hz (clinical standard per **AHA recommendations**).

### Step 2 — Apply Nyquist Theorem

$$
f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical Oversampling

To allow realistic anti-aliasing filter roll-off, KTU/clinical practice uses a **25% safety margin**:
$$
f_s = 1.25 \times 2 f_{max} = 1.25 \times 200 = 250 \text{ Hz}
$$

This is why **250 Hz** is the most common sampling rate in commercial ECG devices (e.g., Philips, GE).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Signal Characteristics Table (KTU Examiner Gold)

| Signal | Frequency Range (Hz) | Amplitude Range | Acquisition Method | Typical Application |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| ECG | 0.05 – 100 | 0.5 – 5 mV | Surface electrodes (Ag/AgCl) | Cardiac monitoring, arrhythmia detection |
| EEG | 0.1 – 100 | 10 – 300 µV | Scalp electrodes (10–20 system) | Epilepsy, sleep staging, BCI |
| EMG | 20 – 2000 | 0.1 – 5 mV | Needle/surface electrodes | Neuromuscular disorders, prosthetics |
| EOG | 0 – 30 | 0.05 – 3.5 mV | Periocular electrodes | Eye-movement tracking, sleep studies |
| ERG | 0.2 – 300 | 0.5 µV – 1 mV | Corneal electrode | Retinal disorders |
| PCG | 20 – 2000 | — | Electronic stethoscope / microphone | Heart-valve auscultation |
| MEG | DC – 100 | fT–pT (magnetic) | SQUID array (cryogenic) | Epilepsy focus localization |
| MCG | 0.05 – 100 | pT | SQUID | Fetal cardiac monitoring |
| Blood Pressure | DC – 50 | mmHg | Sphygmomanometer / catheter | Hypertension management |
| PPG | 0.5 – 10 | 0.1 % – 10 % light absorption | Optical probe | Pulse oximetry, HR estimation |
| Respiration | 0.1 – 10 | Variable (litres/min) | Strain gauge / thermistor | Sleep apnea, COPD |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember this Mnemonic:** *"Every ECG Patient Must Enter Quietly"* →
> **E**CG, **E**EG, **P**CG, **M**EG, **E**MG, **Q**RST-waveforms… (Used by toppers to recall classifications.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Frequently Repeated Board Question)

**Problem:** An ECG is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. Compute (i) Nyquist frequency, (ii) the number of samples in one cardiac cycle of 0.8 s, (iii) maximum analysable frequency without aliasing.

### Solution

**(i) Nyquist Frequency:**
$$
f_{N} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Samples per Cardiac Cycle:**
$$
N = f_s \times T = 500 \times 0.8 = 400 \text{ samples}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Analysable Frequency:**
$$
f_{max} = 250 \text{ Hz}
$$

Since the clinical ECG is band-limited to 100 Hz, the sampling comfortably avoids aliasing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Biomedical Signal Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Living Biological Source] --> B[Transducer Electrode Sensor]
    B --> C[Amplifier LNA INA]
    C --> D[Anti Aliasing Filter 0.05 to 100 Hz]
    D --> E[ADC Sampling at 250 Hz]
    E --> F[Digital Signal Processor]
    F --> G1[Feature Extraction]
    F --> G2[Filtering and Denoising]
    F --> G3[Spectral Analysis FFT]
    F --> G4[Pattern Classification ML]
    G1 --> H[Clinical Diagnosis Output]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Therapy Monitoring Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **canonical answer sketch** for the question *"Draw and explain the block diagram of a biomedical signal acquisition system."*

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree: Biomedical Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Biomedical Signals] --> BioElec[Bioelectric Signals]
    Root --> BioMag[Biomagnetic Signals]
    Root --> BioMech[Biomechanical Signals]
    Root --> BioChem[Biochemical Signals]
    Root --> BioAcou[Bioacoustic Signals]
    Root --> BioOpt[Bio optical Signals]
    BioElec --> ECG[ECG]
    BioElec --> EEG[EEG]
    BioElec --> EMG[EMG]
    BioElec --> EOG[EOG]
    BioElec --> ERG[ERG]
    BioMag --> MCG[MCG]
    BioMag --> MEG[MEG]
    BioMech --> BP[Blood Pressure]
    BioMech --> Flow[Blood Flow]
    BioChem --> pH[pH of Blood]
    BioChem --> pO2[Partial Pressure of Oxygen]
    BioAcou --> PCG[Phonocardiogram]
    BioAcou --> Lung[Lung Sounds]
    BioOpt --> PPG[Photoplethysmogram]
    BioOpt --> PO[Pulse Oximetry]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Waveform Comparison: ECG, EEG, EMG Morphology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECG_Signal[ECG Waveform P QRS T] --> Morph1[Sharp QRS High Amplitude 0.5 to 5 mV]
    EEG_Signal[EEG Waveform Alpha Beta Theta Delta] --> Morph2[Low Amplitude 10 to 300 uV Continuous]
    EMG_Signal[EMG Waveform Motor Unit Action Potentials] --> Morph3[Burst like Pattern 20 to 2000 Hz]
    Morph1 --> Compare[Compare Frequency Content and Use Case]
    Morph2 --> Compare
    Morph3 --> Compare
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Challenges in Biomedical Signal Processing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Biomedical Signal Acquisition Challenges] --> Noise[Noise Sources]
    Start --> Artifact[Motion and Physiological Artifacts]
    Start --> BW[Bandwidth and Resolution Limits]
    Start --> Variability[Inter patient Variability]
    Start --> Safety[Patient Safety Electrical Isolation]
    Noise --> N1[Powerline Interference 50 or 60 Hz]
    Noise --> N2[Thermal Noise Johnson Nyquist]
    Noise --> N3[Shot Noise from Electrodes]
    Artifact --> A1[Baseline Wander due to Respiration]
    Artifact --> A2[EMG Contamination in ECG]
    Artifact --> A3[Eye Blink in EEG]
    BW --> B1[Low Frequency Baseline Drift]
    BW --> B2[High Frequency Aliasing]
    Variability --> V1[Age Gender Pathology Differences]
    Safety --> S1[IEC 60601 1 Standards]
    Safety --> S2[Defibrillation Protection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART — A (3-Mark Questions)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Define a biomedical signal. List any three bioelectric signals with their typical amplitude range.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A *biomedical signal* is a measurable physiological quantity, varying with time or space, that originates from a biological system and conveys information regarding the functional status of that system.

Three bioelectric signals with amplitude ranges:

| Signal | Full Form | Amplitude Range |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| ECG | Electrocardiogram | **0.5 – 5 mV** |
| EEG | Electroencephalogram | **10 – 300 µV** |
| EMG | Electromyogram | **0.1 – 5 mV** |

**Cognitive Level:** Remember (Revised Bloom's L1). **CO Mapping:** CO1.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the minimum sampling rate required for an ECG signal with bandwidth 100 Hz?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** *A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be reconstructed without distortion if and only if it is sampled at a frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.*

For ECG with $f_{max} = 100$ Hz:
$$
f_{s,\min} = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

In clinical practice, a 25 % safety margin is added, giving **$f_s = 250$ Hz** as the universally accepted sampling rate.

**Cognitive Level:** Understand / Apply (Revised Bloom's L2–L3). **CO Mapping:** CO1, CO2.

---

## PART — B (14-Mark Questions — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each sub-question carries **7 marks**. Total = **14 marks**. Choose **either** Option A **or** Option B in the KTU ESE answer booklet.

---

### OPTION A — Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the various stages involved in the acquisition and processing of a biomedical signal. *(b)* Classify biomedical signals with suitable examples. Tabulate the typical frequency and amplitude ranges for any four major bioelectric signals.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The biomedical signal acquisition chain consists of **six sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Source / Patient Interface:**
The biological source (e.g., heart) generates ionic currents. Surface electrodes (typically **Ag/AgCl**) transduce ionic currents into electronic voltages.

**Stage 2 — Preamplification (Low-Noise Instrumentation Amplifier):**
A differential amplifier with high **CMRR** (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio ≥ **100 dB**) and high input impedance (≥ **10 MΩ**) boosts signals of the order of µV to a workable mV range. Example IC: **AD620 / INA128**.

**Stage 3 — Filtering:**
- *Analog anti-aliasing filter* (low-pass Butterworth, cut-off 100 Hz for ECG) to satisfy Nyquist.
- *Notch filter* at **50 Hz** (India) to suppress mains interference.

**Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):**
- Sampling frequency: ≥ **250 Hz** (ECG).
- Resolution: **12-bit to 16-bit**.

**Stage 5 — Digital Signal Processing:**
Operations include — digital filtering (IIR/FIR), FFT-based spectral analysis, wavelet decomposition, PCA/ICA artefact removal.

**Stage 6 — Display / Recording / Decision:**
Output to a monitor, recording medium, or diagnostic decision support system.

*See Section 4.1 for the matching Mermaid block diagram to be reproduced on A4 sheet.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

Biomedical signals are classified as (see Section 4.2 Mermaid tree):

1. **Bioelectric** — ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, ERG
2. **Biomagnetic** — MCG, MEG
3. **Biochemical** — pH, $pO_2$, $pCO_2$
4. **Biomechanical** — BP, blood flow, joint angle
5. **Bioacoustic** — PCG, lung sounds
6. **Bio-optical** — PPG, pulse oximetry

**Tabulated Bioelectric Characteristics:**

| Signal | Frequency Range | Amplitude Range |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| ECG | 0.05 – 100 Hz | 0.5 – 5 mV |
| EEG | 0.1 – 100 Hz | 10 – 300 µV |
| EMG | 20 – 2000 Hz | 0.1 – 5 mV |
| EOG | 0 – 30 Hz | 0.05 – 3.5 mV |

**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyse (L3–L4). **CO Mapping:** CO1, CO2.

---

### OPTION B — Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** *(a)* Explain in detail the challenges encountered in biomedical signal processing. Discuss any three major sources of noise in ECG recording. *(b)* Derive the average signal power of a discrete-time sinusoid $x[n] = A\sin(\omega n)$ and compute the SNR if a noise of RMS amplitude 0.05 mV is added to an ECG of amplitude 1 mV.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Challenges in Biomedical Signal Processing:**

1. **Extremely Low Signal Amplitudes:** EEG signals are of the order of µV, easily buried in thermal noise. Demand high-gain, low-noise amplifiers.
2. **High Source Impedance:** Skin–electrode impedance varies with sweat, hair, dead skin cells → measurement instability.
3. **Frequent Contamination by Physiological Artifacts:**
   - *Baseline Wander* (respiration: 0.15 – 0.5 Hz).
   - *Muscle artefact / EMG* (5 Hz – 2 kHz).
   - *Eye-blink artefact in EEG* (DC – 10 Hz).
4. **Powerline Interference:** Strong 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz coupling — demands notch filter or adaptive cancellation.
5. **Patient Safety & Electrical Isolation:** Must comply with **IEC 60601-1** standards — defibrillation-proof front-end, isolated power supplies.
6. **Inter-subject Variability:** Heart rate, EEG rhythms, muscle mass differ across patients → fixed-threshold algorithms fail.

**Three Major Noise Sources in ECG:**

| Noise Source | Frequency Content | Origin | Remedy |
|--------------|-------------------|--------|--------|
| **Powerline interference** | 50 Hz + harmonics | Mains electromagnetic coupling | Notch / adaptive filter |
| **Baseline wander** | 0.05 – 0.5 Hz | Respiration, electrode movement | High-pass filter (cut-off 0.05 Hz) |
| **EMG (muscle) noise** | 5 Hz – 2 kHz | Patient muscle tremor | Low-pass filter / wavelet denoising |
| **Thermal (Johnson) noise** | White, broadband | Random electron motion in resistors | Lower source resistance, cooled preamp |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (Detailed, as in Section 3.1):**
$$
P = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega n) = \frac{A^{2}}{2}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

- ECG amplitude: $A_{sig} = 1$ mV → signal RMS amplitude $= \dfrac{A_{sig}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$ mV.
- Noise RMS amplitude: $A_{noise} = 0.05$ mV.

**SNR in dB (amplitude form):**
$$
\mathrm{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{sig,rms}}{A_{noise,rms}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.7071}{0.05}\right)
$$

$$
\mathrm{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(14.142) = 20 \times 1.1505 \approx 23.01 \text{ dB}
$$

**Alternative SNR (power form):**
$$
\mathrm{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A^{2}/2}{A_{noise}^{2}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(200) \approx 23.01 \text{ dB}
$$

Both forms yield the same result — **≈ 23 dB** — which is clinically acceptable for diagnostic ECG.

**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyse / Evaluate (L3–L5). **CO Mapping:** CO2, CO3.

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where Students Lose 2–5 Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Writing frequency in "Hz" without units, or amplitude in mV when µV is required → **–1 mark** each occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing SNR formulas:** Using $20 \log$ for power or $10 \log$ for amplitude → **–2 marks**.
> 3. **Missing the "25 % safety margin" on sampling rate:** Examiners specifically deduct marks if $f_s = 200$ Hz is stated without justification of 250 Hz clinical usage.
> 4. **No block diagram / no units in table:** Always pair tabular answers with **units in parentheses** in the header row.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** Writing the final formula without intermediate algebraic steps → examiner awards *partial marks only*.
> 6. **Misclassification:** Classifying PPG as bioacoustic — it is **bio-optical**. This error is **specifically watched** by examiners.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **biomedical signal** is a measurable physiological variable carrying diagnostic information.
- [x] Six primary classifications: **Bioelectric, Biomagnetic, Biochemical, Biomechanical, Bioacoustic, Bio-optical** (+ Bio-thermal).
- [x] ECG bandwidth = **0.05 – 100 Hz**; typical sampling rate = **250 Hz**.
- [x] EEG amplitude = **10 – 300 µV** → demands ultra-low-noise instrumentation amplifiers.
- [x] MEG/MCG magnitudes = **pT–fT** → requires **SQUID** magnetometers.
- [x] Nyquist theorem: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — non-negotiable.
- [x] Average power of discrete sinusoid $A \sin(\omega n)$ = $A^{2}/2$ (frequency-independent).
- [x] SNR: use $10 \log_{10}(P_{s}/P_{n})$ for power ratios; $20 \log_{10}(A_{s}/A_{n})$ for amplitude ratios.
- [x] Acquisition chain: **Source → Electrode → LNA → Anti-alias filter → ADC → DSP → Display**.
- [x] Three major ECG noises: **Powerline (50 Hz), Baseline Wander, EMG artefact**.
- [x] Standard compliance: **IEC 60601-1** for medical-device safety.
- [x] Always include units in every numerical answer and every table header.
- [x] PPG = **bio-optical** (NOT bioacoustic) — examiners repeatedly test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Biomedical Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT633 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Characteristics of Biomedical Signals ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Biomedical Signal** is formally defined as a time-varying or spatially-varying *quantitative physical quantity* that originates from a biological, physiological, or anatomical source and carries diagnostic, therapeutic, or investigative information about the state of a living system. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, biomedical signals are modelled as one-dimensional (temporal) or multi-dimensional (spatio-temporal) electrical, mechanical, chemical, or acoustic manifestations of bioelectric, biomechanical, or biochemical activity. The rigorous mathematical abstraction of a continuous-time biomedical signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as: $$x(t) = s(t) + n(t) + a(t)$$ where $s(t)$ represents the underlying deterministic or stochastic physiological signal of interest, $n(t)$ represents additive stochastic measurement noise, and $a(t)$ represents deterministic artifacts generated by non-physiological sources (e.g., motion, electrode displacement). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The characteristics of biomedical signals cover five primary analytical dimensions — (1) Origin and Nature, (2) Amplitude Range, (3) Frequency Spectrum, (4) Stationarity, and (5) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). Mastery of these five dimensions is mandatory for solving KTU Part A and Part B questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Intuition Imagine a hospital patient monitor at the bedside. The beeping rhythm of the heart monitor is not random — every beep corresponds to an electrical wave travelling through the patient's heart muscle. This electrical wave, when picked up by surface electrodes, produces a voltage waveform called the **ECG (Electrocardiogram)**. Similarly, the squiggly lines on a brain monitor represent voltage oscillations generated by billions of synchronised neurons — the **EEG (Electroencephalogram)**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Body as a Symphony Orchestra":** > The human body can be thought of as an orchestra. Each organ (heart, brain, muscle, eye) is a section of musicians producing its own signal. Biomedical signal processing is the audio engineer who places microphones (sensors), tunes the mixing console (amplifier/filter), and removes the unwanted crowd noise (artifacts) so that the diagnostic melody can be heard clearly. ### 1.3 The Five Cardinal Characteristics Every biomedical signal that a KTU student must analyse possesses the following five defining attributes: **1. Origin of the Signal** — Whether the signal is electrical (e.g., ECG, EEG), mechanical (e.g., blood pressure waveform), acoustic (e.g., PCG — phonocardiogram), or biochemical (e.g., oxygen saturation curve). **2. Amplitude Magnitude** — Biomedical signals span a remarkably wide dynamic range, from **microvolts (µV)** for deep-brain EEG to several **millivolts (mV)** for ECG, representing a 1000× spread. **3. Frequency Content** — The spectral occupancy ranges from quasi-DC (0.01 Hz baseline wander in ECG) up to several kilohertz (2 kHz for EMG and PCG). **4. Stationarity** — Most biomedical signals are *quasi-stationary*: their statistical properties (mean, variance, PSD) remain constant only over short observation windows (typically 10–500 ms). **5. SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** — Defined as the ratio of signal power to noise power. Most surface-recorded biomedical signals suffer from poor SNR in the range of **0 dB to 30 dB**, demanding sophisticated denoising algorithms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants to Memorise for KTU Board Exams:** > - Standard ECG bandwidth (clinical): **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** > - Standard EEG bandwidth: **0.5 Hz – 70 Hz** > - Powerline interference frequency in India: **50 Hz** > - Standard EMG bandwidth: **20 Hz – 2000 Hz** > - Standard body temperature (reference baseline): **37 °C = 310 K** ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Biomedical Signals Biomedical signals can be classified along multiple orthogonal axes. KTU examiners frequently test this classification in 3-mark Part A questions. #### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Determinism **A. Deterministic Signals** — Signals that can be completely described by an explicit mathematical function. Examples: ECG waveform during normal sinus rhythm (can be modelled analytically), evoked response potentials (ERPs). $$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$ **B. Random (Stochastic) Signals** — Signals whose future values cannot be predicted with certainty; only statistical descriptors (mean, autocorrelation, PSD) are defined. Examples: EEG background activity, EMG during sustained contraction, fetal heart rate variability. $$E\{x(t)\} = \mu_x, \quad \text{Var}\{x(t)\} = \sigma_x^2$$ #### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Stationarity **A. Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS)** — All joint statistical moments are invariant under arbitrary time shifts. Strictly speaking, *no biomedical signal is truly SSS*. **B. Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** — Mean is constant and autocorrelation depends only on the time lag $\tau$. $$R_x(t_1, t_2) = R_x(\tau), \quad \text{where } \tau = t_2 - t_1$$ **C. Cyclostationary** — Statistics vary periodically with time. Example: ECG is cyclostationary with the periodicity of the R-R interval. **D. Non-Stationary** — Most real biomedical signals (EEG bursts, seizure activity, EMG fatigue) are inherently non-stationary. #### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Energy / Power **A. Energy Signals** — Have finite total energy $E < \infty$. Evoked potentials (single stimulus response) are typical energy signals. $$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$ **B. Power Signals** — Have finite average power $P < \infty$ but infinite total energy. Continuous ECG monitoring is a power signal. $$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$ #### 2.1.4 Classification Based on Domain of Definition **A. Continuous-Time (Analog) Signals** — Defined for every instant $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Produced directly by biological sources. **B. Discrete-Time Signals** — Defined only at discrete sampling instants $t = nT_s$. Obtained after ADC. **C. Digital Signals** — Discrete in both time and amplitude (quantised). Used in computer-based diagnostic systems. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following consolidated table is the *single most important study sheet* for tackling KTU Board Examination questions on this topic. All quantities use SI units unless explicitly noted. | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Range (Biomedical) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | 0 to 30 | dB | | Power Spectral Density | $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ | Band-limited | V²/Hz | | Autocorrelation (WSS) | $R_x(\tau) = E\{x(t) x(t+\tau)\}$ | $R_x(0) = \sigma_x^2$ | V² | | Total Energy | $E = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Finite for evoked potentials | Joule | | Average Power | $P = \displaystyle\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Finite for periodic signals | Watt | | Root Mean Square (RMS) | $x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\, dt}$ | Used for noise quantification | V | | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | 80 to 120 for medical amplifiers | dB | | Signal Bandwidth | $BW = f_h - f_l$ | ECG: $\approx$ 150 Hz; EEG: $\approx$ 70 Hz | Hz | | Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | ECG: $f_s \geq 500$ Hz | Hz | | Quantisation Noise Power | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | $\Delta$ = LSB voltage | V² | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not merely academic — they underpin the design of every clinical monitor (e.g., GE MAC 5500 ECG, Philips Intellivue patient monitor). A cardiac defibrillator, for example, requires SNR > 40 dB and bandwidth > 100 Hz to reliably detect ventricular fibrillation. ### 2.3 Real-World Significance in Clinical Engineering In the hospital environment, biomedical signal characteristics dictate three critical engineering design decisions: 1. **Electrode/Sensor Selection** — Silver/Silver-Chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrodes are preferred for ECG due to their low half-cell potential ($\approx$ 220 mV) and stable DC offset. 2. **Amplifier Topology** — Instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., INA128, AD620) with CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB are mandated to reject 50 Hz powerline interference. 3. **ADC Specifications** — Resolution of 12-bit minimum (4096 levels) for ECG; 16-bit for EEG (where µV-level signals must be resolved to ~0.15 µV precision). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: SNR Expression from First Principles **Problem:** Given a noisy ECG recording $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, where $s(t)$ is the clean cardiac signal and $n(t)$ is zero-mean additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) with variance $\sigma_n^2$, derive the expression for instantaneous and average SNR. **Step 1 — Definition of instantaneous power at time $t$:** $$P_s(t) = s^2(t), \qquad P_n(t) = n^2(t)$$ **Step 2 — Time-averaged signal power over an observation window of duration $T$:** $$\overline{P_s} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T s^2(t) \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Time-averaged noise power (using variance for zero-mean WSS noise):** $$\overline{P_n} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T n^2(t) \, dt = \sigma_n^2$$ **Step 4 — Definition of linear SNR:** $$\text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = \frac{\overline{P_s}}{\overline{P_n}} = \frac{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T s^2(t)\, dt}{\sigma_n^2}$$ **Step 5 — Conversion to logarithmic (decibel) scale used in all medical instrumentation specifications:** $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\overline{P_s}}{\overline{P_n}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\int_0^T s^2(t)\, dt}{T \sigma_n^2}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Example numerical evaluation:** Let the clean ECG have RMS amplitude $1$ mV over a window of $T = 1$ s, and noise RMS be $0.1$ mV. Then: $$\overline{P_s} = (1 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{V}^2$$ $$\overline{P_n} = (0.1 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{V}^2$$ $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-8}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \; \text{dB}$$ **Conclusion:** The SNR is **20 dB**, meaning the signal power is 100× the noise power. In clinical practice, this is the minimum acceptable SNR for diagnostic-grade ECG recording. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Spectral Density for a Deterministic Periodic ECG **Problem:** The ECG's R-wave can be approximated as a periodic train of Gaussian-shaped pulses. Derive its PSD. **Step 1 — Mathematical model of one ECG R-wave (single Gaussian pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $\sigma$):** $$g(t) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Periodic extension with fundamental period $T_0$ (R-R interval, typically 0.6–1.2 s):** $$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} g(t - k T_0)$$ **Step 3 — Fourier series representation (since $x(t)$ is periodic):** $$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n \, e^{j 2\pi n f_0 t}, \quad f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}$$ **Step 4 — Fourier coefficients via inner product:** $$c_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} g(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi n f_0 t} \, dt$$ **Step 5 — Solving the integral using the Gaussian Fourier pair $\mathcal{F}\{e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}\} = \sigma\sqrt{2\pi}\, e^{-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 f^2}$:** $$c_n = \frac{A \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}}{T_0} \, e^{-2\pi^2 \sigma^2 n^2 f_0^2}$$ **Step 6 — Power Spectral Density (line spectrum since the signal is periodic):** $$S_x(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert c_n \vert^2 \, \delta(f - n f_0) = \frac{2\pi A^2 \sigma^2}{T_0^2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-4\pi^2 \sigma^2 n^2 f_0^2} \, \delta(f - n f_0)$$ **Step 7 — Physical interpretation:** $$\text{Total Power} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_x(f)\, df = \sum_{n} \vert c_n \vert^2 = \frac{A^2 \sigma \sqrt{\pi}}{T_0}$$ This derivation is critical for KTU because it justifies why ECG energy concentrates below 40 Hz (the Gaussian's rapid spectral roll-off $\propto e^{-f^2}$). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quantisation Noise Power in Biomedical ADC **Problem:** An ECG signal with peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{pp} = 5$ mV is digitised by an $N$-bit ADC. Derive the quantisation noise power. **Step 1 — Quantisation step (Least Significant Bit voltage):** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{2^N}$$ **Step 2 — Assumption: quantisation error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$:** $$e_q \sim \mathcal{U}(-\Delta/2, \, \Delta/2)$$ **Step 3 — Mean of quantisation noise (zero, by symmetry):** $$E\{e_q\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \frac{e_q}{\Delta}\, de_q = 0$$ **Step 4 — Variance (noise power) of uniform distribution:** $$\sigma_q^2 = E\{e_q^2\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \frac{e_q^2}{\Delta}\, de_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the LSB voltage expression:** $$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{12}\left(\frac{V_{pp}}{2^N}\right)^2 = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical example for 12-bit ADC ($N = 12$, $V_{pp} = 5$ mV):** $$\Delta = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{4096} = 1.22 \; \mu\text{V}$$ $$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{(1.22 \times 10^{-6})^2}{12} = 1.24 \times 10^{-13} \; \text{V}^2$$ $$\sigma_q = 0.353 \; \mu\text{V (RMS)}$$ **Step 7 — Practical implication:** This is why 12-bit ADC is sufficient for ECG (noise floor $\sim 0.35$ µV is well below thermal noise of skin-electrode interface $\sim 1$ µV). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to derive noise power, *always* state the assumption of uniform quantisation error distribution explicitly. Examiners award 1 mark for this assumption. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Biomedical Signals The following matrix consolidates the characteristic parameters that KTU examiners frequently test in comparative questions: | Signal | Source Organ | Amplitude Range | Frequency Range | Nature | SNR (Typical) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | ECG | Heart muscle | 0.5 – 5 mV | 0.05 – 150 Hz | Cyclostationary, quasi-periodic | 20 – 40 dB | | EEG | Brain (cortex) | 10 – 100 µV | 0.5 – 70 Hz | Stochastic, non-stationary | 5 – 20 dB | | EMG | Skeletal muscle | 50 µV – 5 mV | 20 – 2000 Hz | Stochastic, transient | 10 – 30 dB | | PCG | Heart valves | 0.1 – 10 Pa (acoustic) | 20 – 2000 Hz | Quasi-periodic | 15 – 35 dB | | EOG | Eye (corneo-retinal) | 10 µV – 5 mV | 0 – 100 Hz | Piecewise-stationary | 20 – 30 dB | | ERG | Retina | 0.5 µV – 1 mV | 0.2 – 200 Hz | Transient (flash-evoked) | 0 – 15 dB | | Blood Pressure (BP) | Arterial wall | 80 – 200 mmHg | 0 – 60 Hz | Cyclostationary | 30 – 50 dB | | Respiration | Lungs/chest | $\pm 5$ cm H₂O | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Cyclostationary | 25 – 40 dB | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Biomedical Signal Acquisition Chain The complete flow from physiological event to diagnostic display is illustrated below. This block diagram is a high-yield visual that KTU examiners frequently include in their question papers. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physiological Event
Heartbeat, Brain Activity, Muscle Contraction] --> B[Transducer or Electrode
Ag or AgCl Electrode] B --> C[Preamplifier Stage
Instrumentation Amplifier INA128] C --> D[Bandpass Filter Stage
0.05 Hz to 150 Hz for ECG] D --> E[Notch Filter Stage
50 Hz Powerline Rejection] E --> F[Main Amplifier Stage
Gain 1000x Variable] F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter
12 bit 500 Hz Sampling] G --> H[Digital Signal Processor
DSP or Microcontroller] H --> I[Display or Storage Unit
LCD Monitor or SD Card] J[Noise and Artifact Sources] -.->|Coupling Interference| C J -.->|Electromagnetic Pickup| D J -.->|Motion Artifact| B ``` ### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Characteristics Classification Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Biomedical Signal] --> S1[Based on Determinism] S --> S2[Based on Stationarity] S --> S3[Based on Energy Type] S --> S4[Based on Continuity] S1 --> S1a[Deterministic
e.g. Evoked Potential] S1 --> S1b[Random or Stochastic
e.g. EEG Background] S2 --> S2a[Wide Sense Stationary
Short Window EEG] S2 --> S2b[Cyclostationary
e.g. ECG] S2 --> S2c[Non Stationary
e.g. Seizure EEG] S3 --> S3a[Energy Signal
Finite Total Energy] S3 --> S3b[Power Signal
Finite Average Power] S4 --> S4a[Continuous Time
Analog Signal] S4 --> S4b[Discrete Time
Sampled Signal] S4 --> S4c[Digital Signal
Quantised in Amplitude] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Noise Sources Affecting Biomedical Signals ```mermaid flowchart TB N[Noise Sources in Biomedical Recording] --> N1[Internal Physiological Noise] N --> N2[External Environmental Noise] N --> N3[Instrumentation Noise] N1 --> N1a[Muscle Artifact EMG Contamination] N1 --> N1b[Baseline Wander Respiration Drift] N1 --> N1c[Electrodermal Activity Skin Potential] N2 --> N2a[Powerline Interference 50 Hz India] N2 --> N2b[Electromagnetic Interference from Mobile Phones] N2 --> N2c[Radio Frequency Pickup from Surgical Equipment] N3 --> N3a[Thermal Johnson Noise from Electrode Resistance] N3 --> N3b[Shot Noise from Amplifier Input Transistors] N3 --> N3c[Flicker Noise at Low Frequencies below 10 Hz] ``` ### 4.4 Frequency Spectrum Band Diagram — EEG Bands ```mermaid flowchart LR F0[Frequency Axis 0 to 100 Hz] --> F1[Delta Band 0.5 to 4 Hz Deep Sleep] F1 --> F2[Theta Band 4 to 8 Hz Drowsiness] F2 --> F3[Alpha Band 8 to 13 Hz Relaxed Wakefulness] F3 --> F4[Beta Band 13 to 30 Hz Active Thinking] F4 --> F5[Gamma Band 30 to 70 Hz Cognitive Processing] ``` ### 4.5 Signal Processing Pipeline — From Raw Recording to Clean Output ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Raw Noisy Signal
x of t equals s plus n plus a] --> P1[Preprocessing
Baseline Removal High Pass] P1 --> P2[Noise Removal
Notch Filter 50 Hz] P2 --> P3[Feature Extraction
QRS Detection for ECG] P3 --> P4[Classification or Diagnosis
Normal vs Arrhythmia] R -.->|Artifacts| A1[Artifact Rejection
ICA or Wavelet Thresholding] A1 --> P2 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (Short Answer) **Q: Define a biomedical signal. List any four major biomedical signals with their typical amplitude ranges.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A biomedical signal is a time-varying electrical, mechanical, acoustic, or chemical quantity that originates from a biological source and carries diagnostic information about the physiological state of the body. Four major biomedical signals with amplitude ranges: 1. **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** 0.5 – 5 mV 2. **EEG (Electroencephalogram):** 10 – 100 µV 3. **EMG (Electromyogram):** 50 µV – 5 mV 4. **PCG (Phonocardiogram):** 0.1 – 10 Pa (acoustic pressure) **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correct amplitude ranges (0.5 each). --- #### Question 2 (Short Answer) **Q: Distinguish between a deterministic signal and a random signal. Give one biomedical example of each.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Deterministic Signal | Random Signal | |---|---|---| | Definition | Can be described by an explicit mathematical equation | Cannot be predicted; described only by statistics | | Future value | Known exactly for all $t$ | Known only in probabilistic sense | | Example (Biomedical) | Evoked Response Potential (ERP) after stimulus | EEG background activity | Mark scheme: 1 mark definition (deterministic) + 1 mark definition (random) + 1 mark correct biomedical example. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice) #### **Question A — Set 1 (14 Marks)** **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major sources of noise encountered during biomedical signal acquisition. Discuss how powerline interference can be effectively rejected. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The ECG signal of a patient is recorded with a peak-to-peak amplitude of 4 mV. The RMS noise voltage measured during the isoelectric baseline is 80 µV. Calculate the SNR in dB. If this signal is digitised using a 10-bit ADC with full-scale range of 5 mV, determine the quantisation noise power. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` ##### Model Solution to (a): **Block diagram of noise sources** (refer to Section 4.3 diagram above): The three primary categories are: **1. Internal Physiological Noise:** - EMG contamination from adjacent muscle groups - Baseline wander due to respiration (0.15 – 0.5 Hz) - Electrodermal skin potential variation **2. External Environmental Noise:** - 50 Hz powerline interference (dominant in India) - Electromagnetic interference from mobile phones, Wi-Fi routers - RF interference from electrosurgical units **3. Instrumentation Noise:** - Thermal Johnson-Nyquist noise: $\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \Delta f$ - Shot noise in transistor input stage - Flicker (1/f) noise dominant below 10 Hz **Powerline Interference Rejection — Two Complementary Methods:** (i) **Notch Filter (Hardware):** A second-order active notch filter centred at $f_0 = 50$ Hz with quality factor $Q \geq 10$ to achieve $> 40$ dB attenuation at 50 Hz while preserving ECG frequencies (0.05–40 Hz). $$\vert H(f) \vert^2 = \frac{1 + (f/f_0)^2}{1 + Q^2(f/f_0)^2 + (f/f_0)^4}, \quad f_0 = 50 \; \text{Hz}$$ (ii) **Driven-Right-Leg (DRL) Circuit:** A feedback loop that senses the common-mode voltage on the body and injects an opposing current back into the patient, actively cancelling the common-mode interference. Reduces 50 Hz pickup by 20–40 dB. ##### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Compute signal power from peak-to-peak amplitude:** Assuming sinusoid: $V_{\text{peak}} = V_{pp}/2 = 2$ mV; $V_{\text{rms}} = V_{\text{peak}}/\sqrt{2} = 1.414$ mV. $$\overline{P_s} = V_{\text{rms}}^2 = (1.414 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{V}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute noise power:** $$\overline{P_n} = (80 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{V}^2$$ **Step 3 — SNR in dB:** $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{6.4 \times 10^{-9}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(312.5) = 24.95 \; \text{dB} \approx 25 \; \text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — Quantisation step (LSB) for 10-bit ADC with $V_{FS} = 5$ mV:** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1024} = 4.88 \; \mu\text{V}$$ **Step 5 — Quantisation noise power:** $$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(4.88 \times 10^{-6})^2}{12} = \frac{2.38 \times 10^{-11}}{12} = 1.985 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{V}^2$$ $$\sigma_q = \sqrt{1.985 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.41 \; \mu\text{V (RMS)}$$ **Final Answer:** SNR $\approx$ **25 dB**, Quantisation noise power $\approx$ **1.99 × 10⁻¹² V²**. **Mark Split for (b):** SNR calculation (3 marks) + ADC LSB (2 marks) + Noise power (2 marks). --- #### **Question B — Set 2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **Q: (a)** Classify biomedical signals based on (i) determinism, (ii) stationarity, and (iii) energy/power nature. Provide one example for each category. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Power Spectral Density of a periodic rectangular pulse train used to model a pacemaker stimulus signal. The pulse has amplitude $A = 5$ V, width $\tau = 1$ ms, and repetition period $T_0 = 1$ s. Compute the DC component and the magnitude of the first 3 harmonics. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` ##### Model Solution to (a): **(i) Determinism:** - Deterministic: Fully predictable. *Example:* Single-fibre action potential (modelled by Hodgkin-Huxley equations). - Random (Stochastic): Probabilistic description. *Example:* Surface EEG during resting state. **(ii) Stationarity:** - Wide-Sense Stationary: Mean constant, autocorrelation depends only on lag. *Example:* Short-window (1 s) EEG. - Cyclostationary: Periodic statistics. *Example:* ECG during regular sinus rhythm. - Non-Stationary: Statistics change with time. *Example:* EEG during an epileptic seizure. **(iii) Energy vs Power:** - Energy Signal: $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt < \infty$. *Example:* Single evoked response potential. - Power Signal: $\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt < \infty$. *Example:* Continuous ECG monitoring. ##### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Single rectangular pulse of width $\tau = 1$ ms and amplitude $A = 5$ V:** $$p(t) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert t \vert \leq \tau/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Periodic extension:** $$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} p(t - k T_0), \quad T_0 = 1 \; \text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Fourier series coefficients (using sinc function):** $$c_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} p(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi n f_0 t} \, dt = \frac{A \tau}{T_0} \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n \tau}{T_0}\right)$$ **Step 4 — DC component ($n = 0$):** $$c_0 = \frac{A \tau}{T_0} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V} = 5 \; \text{mV}$$ **Step 5 — First 3 harmonics:** For $n = 1$: $\text{sinc}(\tau/T_0) = \text{sinc}(0.001) \approx \sin(\pi \times 0.001)/(\pi \times 0.001) \approx 1$ $$\vert c_1 \vert = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{\sin(\pi \times 0.001)}{\pi \times 0.001} \approx 4.999995 \; \text{mV}$$ For $n = 2$: $\text{sinc}(2 \times 0.001) \approx 0.99998$ $$\vert c_2 \vert \approx 4.9999 \; \text{mV}$$ For $n = 3$: $\text{sinc}(3 \times 0.001) \approx 0.99996$ $$\vert c_3 \vert \approx 4.9998 \; \text{mV}$$ **Step 6 — General expression for PSD (line spectrum):** $$S_x(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert c_n \vert^2 \, \delta(f - n f_0)$$ **Final Answer:** DC component = **5 mV**; Harmonics $\vert c_1 \vert \approx \vert c_2 \vert \approx \vert c_3 \vert \approx 5$ mV (because $\tau \ll T_0$ makes the signal appear quasi-continuous). **Mark Split for (b):** Derivation of Fourier coefficient (3 marks) + DC computation (2 marks) + Harmonic magnitudes (2 marks). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Confusing SNR formula** — Some students write $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ for *amplitude* comparison. The correct formula for *power* ratio is $10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$. Using 20 instead of 10 loses **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions in quantisation noise derivation** — The uniform distribution assumption on the quantisation error is mandatory. Omission costs **1 mark**. > 3. **Mixing up amplitude units** — ECG is in mV, EEG is in µV. Numerical answers that mix these (e.g., comparing ECG peak 5 mV with noise 5 µV) without unit conversion lose **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Incorrect classification examples** — Listing ECG as "random" or EEG as "deterministic" is wrong and costs **1 mark** per misclassification. > 5. **Missing CMRR mention** — When asked about powerline rejection, many students forget to mention the Driven-Right-Leg circuit or the CMRR specification ($\geq 100$ dB), losing **2 marks**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - [ ] A **biomedical signal** is a time-varying physiological quantity carrying diagnostic information, mathematically modelled as $x(t) = s(t) + n(t) + a(t)$. - [ ] **Five characteristics** to always discuss: Origin, Amplitude, Frequency, Stationarity, SNR. - [ ] **ECG:** 0.5–5 mV, 0.05–150 Hz, cyclostationary. - [ ] **EEG:** 10–100 µV, 0.5–70 Hz, non-stationary stochastic. - [ ] **EMG:** 50 µV–5 mV, 20–2000 Hz, stochastic transient. - [ ] **PCG:** Acoustic, 20–2000 Hz. - [ ] **EOG:** Eye movement, 0–100 Hz. - [ ] **Powerline frequency in India:** 50 Hz (in USA/Europe it is 60 Hz). - [ ] **SNR formula:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$. - [ ] **CMRR for medical amplifiers:** $\geq 100$ dB (instrumentation amp INA128/AD620). - [ ] **Quantisation noise power:** $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ where $\Delta = V_{pp}/2^N$. - [ ] **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist criterion). - [ ] **Three noise categories:** Physiological, Environmental, Instrumentation. - [ ] **Driven-Right-Leg circuit** is the gold-standard hardware method to reject common-mode 50 Hz interference. - [ ] **Quasi-stationarity** — Most biomedical signals are stationary only over short (10–500 ms) windows. - [ ] **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** links autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ to PSD $S_x(f)$ via Fourier transform — frequently tested as a 2-mark Part A item. - [ ] **Power vs Energy classification:** ECG continuous monitoring = Power signal; Single evoked potential = Energy signal. - [ ] **Deterministic vs Random:** Use probability/statistics only for the latter; deterministic signals admit exact Fourier/PSD descriptions. > **🎯 Final Exam Mantra:** "Origin → Amplitude → Frequency → Stationarity → SNR" — recite these five pillars for every biomedical signal you discuss in the KTU answer sheet. Examiners reward structured, dimensional thinking.
